name: Software Development
playbook:
## Picking a model for coding

For day-to-day feature work, prioritize models with strong **agentic tool use** — the ability to read a codebase, run commands, and iterate on failures without hand-holding. For one-off snippets or algorithm questions, raw reasoning quality matters more than tool use.

**Workflow tips:**
- Give the model the actual error message and relevant file, not a paraphrase.
- Ask for a plan before code on anything touching more than 2-3 files.
- Use a second model (or a fresh session) to review a diff before you merge it — a change that looks right to its author often isn't.

**Budget note:** coding sessions burn tokens fast once a model starts reading files. If you're on metered API pricing, watch input costs on large context windows more than output costs.
redFlags: Trusting generated code to be secure without review — models happily produce SQL injection and XSS patterns if asked naively
Letting an agentic tool run destructive shell commands unattended
Assuming a model's training data includes the newest version of a fast-moving framework or API

name: Writing & Content
playbook:
## Picking a model for writing

Models differ more in **voice** than in raw capability for writing tasks. Test 2-3 models on a short sample in your actual voice before committing to one for a big project.

**Workflow tips:**
- Paste 2-3 paragraphs of your own past writing as a style reference before asking for new content.
- Ask for multiple structural outlines before drafting prose — it's cheaper to fix structure than sentences.
- Explicitly ask the model to avoid its own tics (excessive hedging, repetitive sentence openers, over-use of certain transition words).
redFlags: Publishing factual claims (stats, quotes, dates) without independent verification
Letting default AI phrasing patterns ship unedited — readers increasingly notice
Using one long unstructured prompt instead of iterating in stages (outline, draft, edit)

name: Business & Strategy
playbook:
## Picking a model for business use

Most business use cases are read-heavy (long documents, spreadsheets, reports) rather than write-heavy. Prioritize context window size and summarization accuracy over creative flair.

**Workflow tips:**
- For financial or numeric analysis, always ask the model to show its calculation steps and spot-check at least one number by hand.
- Break multi-part strategic questions into separate turns rather than one mega-prompt — you'll get more scrutiny on each part.
- Use the model to generate a first-draft structure for decks/memos, but keep judgment calls (what to prioritize, what to cut) as a human step.
redFlags: Feeding confidential or regulated data into a consumer-tier chat product without checking your organization's data policy
Treating model-generated financial projections as verified rather than a starting draft
Asking one giant open-ended question instead of a scoped, checkable one

name: Education & Learning
criteria: Explaining concepts at the right level
Generating practice problems with correct answers
Avoiding just handing over answers when learning is the goal
Multimodal support (images of handwritten work, diagrams)
playbook:
## Picking a model for learning

The best study-tool model isn't necessarily the most capable one — it's the one you can instruct to act like a tutor instead of an answer key.

**Workflow tips:**
- Explicitly prompt: "Don't give me the final answer — ask me guiding questions until I get there."
- Ask for a worked example first, then a similar problem to try solo, then have the model check your work.
- For visual subjects (math diagrams, chemistry structures, handwriting), use a model with strong image understanding.
redFlags: Using AI to complete graded work in violation of your institution's academic integrity policy
Accepting a confident-sounding explanation of a niche topic without cross-checking a textbook or teacher
Letting the model do all the problem-solving instead of guiding you through it

name: Healthcare & Wellness
criteria: Ability to explain lab results or terminology in plain language
Citing when something needs a licensed professional
Privacy handling of sensitive personal data
playbook:
## Using AI around health topics

Treat every model here as a **research assistant for questions to bring to a professional**, not a diagnostic tool. The best models for this use case are the ones that reliably flag their own limits rather than the ones that sound most confident.

**Workflow tips:**
- Ask the model to explain lab results, medication interactions, or terminology in plain language — this is a genuinely strong use case.
- Avoid pasting personally identifying health information into consumer chat products; use privacy-respecting settings or de-identify details first.
- Always follow up anything that sounds like a diagnosis or treatment recommendation with a licensed provider.
redFlags: Using any model's output as a substitute for professional medical diagnosis or emergency care
Pasting identifiable patient data into a consumer product without checking privacy/compliance implications
Trusting a confident tone as a proxy for medical accuracy
